blocks.md:
## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one flow at a time — ingest, store, search, alert — then go deep on the two or three hardest parts: the ingestion firehose, the storage tiering, and search at scale. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: a logging system is a **write-heavy firehose** (millions of tiny events a second from thousands of services) that must *also* be **instantly searchable** (an on-call engineer typing a query at 3am during an outage). Optimizing one usually hurts the other — that's the whole game here.

🏢 I'll target an **Amazon SDE-3 bar**: cost and operations are first-class, and I'll work backwards from the customer (the on-call engineer who needs answers fast). Tell me if you'd rather I tilt elsewhere.

### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")

**1. Ingest logs from many services with minimal latency.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: any app or service in the company can fire off a log line ("user 42 checkout failed") and our system swallows it instantly without slowing that app down.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: thousands of producers sending tiny messages at ~100K/s means the *act of accepting* a log can't block the caller or lose data when a downstream piece is slow.

**2. Aggregate and durably store logs.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: all those scattered log lines from hundreds of machines get collected into one place and kept safely, so nothing is lost even if a server dies.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: at ~4TB/day raw you can't keep everything hot forever — you need cheap durable storage *and* a story for what's queryable vs archived.

**3. Search and filter logs.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: an engineer can type "show me all 500 errors from the payments service in the last 15 minutes" and get results in about a second.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: full-text + structured search over billions of rows in sub-second time is exactly what a plain database can't do — you need an inverted index.

**4. Analyze and alert on logs.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: the system can watch the stream and automatically ping someone when, say, error rate spikes past a threshold — without a human staring at a screen.
- ⚡ Why it's hard: alerting means running continuous aggregations over a moving stream, not a one-off query, and it must fire within seconds to be useful.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** log *parsing/normalization* of arbitrary vendor formats, distributed tracing correlation (we'll keep a `trace_id` field but not build the tracing UI), RBAC down to per-field redaction, and ML-based anomaly detection (we'll do threshold alerts; ML is a follow-on).

### Non-Functional requirements

**1. Availability of the ingest path — target 99.99% ("four nines").**
- 🗣️ Plain words: the part that *accepts* logs is down less than ~1 hour per year total.
- 💥 What breaks without it: if ingestion is down during an incident, we're blind exactly when we most need logs — the outage we're debugging has no data.

**2. Durability — target ~99.999999% (eight nines), zero acknowledged-log loss.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: once we say "got it" to a producer, that log will essentially never be lost.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a dropped log during a security incident means a missing audit trail — you literally cannot prove what happened.

**3. Ingest latency — producer-side enqueue p99 < 10ms; end-to-end (ingest → searchable) < ~30s.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: writing a log feels instant to the app; it becomes searchable within about half a minute.
- 💥 What breaks without it: if logging blocks the app, we've made every service slower just by observing it — the classic "monitoring takes down prod" failure.

**4. Search latency — p99 < ~2s for a 15-minute window over recent (hot) data.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: the on-call engineer's query comes back in a couple of seconds, not a coffee break.
- 💥 What breaks without it: slow search during an outage means longer downtime — search latency is incident-resolution latency.

**5. Scalability — linear scale to ~100K logs/s sustained, ~300K/s peak, with headroom to 1M/s.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: when the company doubles its services, we add machines and it just keeps working.
- 💥 What breaks without it: a fixed-capacity design falls over on the worst day (a cascading outage produces a *log storm* — error logging spikes exactly when load spikes).

**6. Cost-efficiency — storage cost dominated by cheap object storage, not hot indexes.**
- 🗣️ Plain words: we don't pay premium prices to keep year-old logs instantly searchable when almost nobody queries them.
- 💥 What breaks without it: keeping everything in a hot search cluster would cost 10–50x more than tiering to object storage — the bill, not the tech, kills the project.

⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud: I'm assuming **at-least-once** delivery (so logs can be duplicated and we dedupe), not exactly-once — I'll revisit in the ledger. And I'm treating logs as **append-only and non-PII by default**; if logs carry PII we'd need field-level redaction, which I'm scoping out.

> 💬 "The thing I keep front-of-mind: this is a write firehose that has to double as a search engine. Those two pull in opposite directions, so almost every decision is me trading write throughput against query speed and cost."

> 🎙️ Script: "So at its heart this is a system that has to do two hard things at once. It has to swallow a firehose of tiny log events from thousands of services without ever blocking them or losing data — that's a durability and throughput problem. And it has to turn around and let an engineer search billions of those events in about a second during an outage — that's a latency and indexing problem. My non-functionals are basically: never lose an acknowledged log, never slow down the apps that log, stay up during incidents, and don't go bankrupt on storage. Everything downstream is me balancing those four."

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**1. "Roughly how many services produce logs, and what's the aggregate log rate at peak?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: the whole architecture hinges on the firehose size — 1K logs/s is a single box, 1M/s is a sharded distributed system.
- ↔️ Design fork: If LOW (<10K/s) → a single Kafka topic + one Elasticsearch cluster is plenty. If HIGH (>100K/s) → partitioned ingest, tiered storage, and search sharding are mandatory.
- Assumption: ~10K services, ~100K logs/s sustained, ~300K/s peak.

**2. "What's the retention requirement — how far back must logs be instantly searchable vs merely archived?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: hot retention drives the single biggest cost line; if we only need 7 days hot, the search cluster shrinks 4x.
- ↔️ Design fork: If SHORT (7–30 days hot) → small hot tier + cheap cold archive. If LONG (everything searchable for a year) → either a huge expensive cluster or a slower searchable-archive tier.
- Assumption: 30 days hot/searchable, 1 year warm (searchable but slower), then delete.

**3. "Is at-least-once delivery acceptable, or do we need exactly-once (no duplicate log lines ever)?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: exactly-once across a distributed pipeline is enormously more expensive; logs usually tolerate dedupe.
- ↔️ Design fork: If at-least-once → simple, fast, dedupe by event id on read. If exactly-once → idempotent consumers + transactional writes everywhere, big throughput cost.
- Assumption: at-least-once with a unique `log_id` so duplicates are detectable.

**4. "How structured are the logs — free-text lines, or structured JSON with known fields?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: structured logs index cleanly and query fast; free text needs heavier full-text indexing.
- ↔️ Design fork: If STRUCTURED JSON → index specific fields, cheaper. If FREE TEXT → full inverted index on the message body, larger index.
- Assumption: mostly structured JSON (timestamp, service, level, message, trace_id, host) with a free-text `message` field.

**5. "What query patterns dominate — recent tail-following, or deep historical analytics?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: tailing recent logs and running year-long aggregations are different engines.
- ↔️ Design fork: If RECENT-heavy → optimize the hot tier and time-bucketed indexes. If ANALYTICS-heavy → add a columnar OLAP store (ClickHouse) for aggregations.
- Assumption: 90% queries hit the last 24h; alerting + tailing dominate.

**6. "Do we need real-time alerting, and what's the acceptable alert latency?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: alerting within seconds requires a stream processor; minutes can be done by periodic queries.
- ↔️ Design fork: If SECONDS → stream processing (Flink) on the live pipeline. If MINUTES → a cron that queries the index.
- Assumption: alerts within ~10–30s via stream processing.

**7. "Single region or global? Do logs need to stay in-region for compliance?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: multi-region changes durability, latency, and data-residency design.
- ↔️ Design fork: If SINGLE → simpler, one pipeline. If GLOBAL → regional ingest + regional storage with optional central aggregation.
- Assumption: regional ingest (logs stay in-region), single primary query plane per region.

**8. "What's the average log line size?"**
- 🗣️ Why I'm asking: it drives bandwidth and storage math directly.
- ↔️ Design fork: If SMALL (~200B) → cheaper. If LARGE (stack traces, ~2KB) → 4–10x storage and bandwidth.
- Assumption: ~500 bytes average per log line.

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "Those are my assumptions — ~100K logs/s, 30-day hot retention, at-least-once, structured JSON, real-time alerting. Are any of those wrong before I size it?"

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

Gloss first: **QPS** = queries (or events) per second; **ingest rate** = log events accepted per second; **hot tier** = recent data kept in fast searchable storage; **cold/warm tier** = older data on cheap object storage.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Avg ingest rate | 10K services × 10 logs/s each = 100,000/s | ~100K/s | "about a hundred thousand logs a second" | Must partition ingest — past one broker/node |
| Peak ingest rate | 100K/s × 3 (incident burst factor) = 300,000/s | ~300K/s | "spikes to three hundred thousand" | Buffer (Kafka) must absorb 3x without backpressure to apps |
| Avg bandwidth | 100K/s × 500 bytes = 50,000,000 B/s | ~50 MB/s | "about 50 megabytes a second" | Network + broker throughput sizing |
| Peak bandwidth | 300K/s × 500 B = 150,000,000 B/s | ~150 MB/s | "150 MB/s at peak" | ~2 Kafka brokers cover it at 100MB/s each → run more for HA |
| Logs/day | 100K/s × 86,400 s = 8.64 billion | ~8.6B/day | "almost 9 billion log lines a day" | Index can't keep all of it hot |
| Raw storage/day | 8.64B × 500 B = 4.32 TB | ~4.3 TB/day | "a bit over 4 terabytes a day raw" | Compression + tiering mandatory |
| Compressed/day | 4.3 TB ÷ 10 (logs compress ~10:1) = 432 GB | ~430 GB/day | "about 430 gigs a day compressed" | Real storage planning number |
| Hot tier (30d, 3x replicas) | 430 GB × 30 × 3 = 38,700 GB | ~39 TB | "roughly 40 terabytes hot" | Search cluster sizing + cost |
| Warm tier (1yr on object store, ~1.3x EC) | 430 GB × 365 × 1.3 = 204 TB | ~205 TB | "about 200 terabytes warm for a year" | Goes to S3-class storage, not the cluster |
| Search QPS | ~few hundred engineers + dashboards | ~200 QPS | "a couple hundred searches a second" | Tiny vs ingest — reads are NOT the bottleneck |

The ONE number that forces the design: **~300K logs/s at peak.** A single Kafka broker sustains ~100MB/s and a single Elasticsearch node ingests ~tens of thousands of docs/s — so the flip threshold is roughly **>50K docs/s, at which point both the buffer and the index must be partitioned/sharded.** We're 6x past it, so partitioning isn't optional.

Storage's dominant cost is the **hot search index (~40TB on fast SSD-backed nodes)**, not the warm archive — the archive is ~200TB but on object storage at ~1/20th the price per GB. That's the cost lever.

> 🎙️ Script: "The numbers that matter: we're ingesting about a hundred thousand logs a second, spiking to three hundred thousand during incidents. That's well past what one machine or one search node can take, so I'm partitioning everything from the start. Reads are almost nothing by comparison — a couple hundred searches a second — so I'm spending my entire budget on the write path and on storage tiering, not on read scaling. And the cost story is simple: keep 30 days hot and searchable, push everything older to cheap object storage."

## 4. Core Entities

> 🎙️ I'd start by talking about the key entities and why each one exists — this is a first draft; I'll add full field definitions in the data model in §7.

**LogEvent** — the atomic unit: one structured log line from one service at one moment. Owns the actual payload.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: think of it like one line in a giant diary — a timestamp, who wrote it, and what happened.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you dedupe these if delivery is at-least-once?" → each LogEvent carries a `log_id` (producer-generated UUID + timestamp); the index treats it as the document id, so a replay overwrites rather than duplicates.

**Source / Service** — metadata about who is producing logs (service name, host, environment, region).
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: like the "from" address on an envelope — tells you which app and which machine sent the line.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you handle a brand-new service with no config?" → sources self-register on first log; we tag with defaults and an `unverified` flag rather than dropping.

**Index / TimeBucket** — a logical grouping of LogEvents by time window and service, the unit we search, roll over, and age out.
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: like monthly folders in a filing cabinet — you don't search every drawer, you go straight to "payments, last hour."
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "Why time-bucketed indexes?" → so we can drop an entire old index in O(1) at retention time and so queries prune to only the buckets in the time range.

**AlertRule** — a user-defined condition over the stream (e.g. "error rate for payments > 100/min").
- 🗣️ Simple analogy: like a smoke detector — it watches continuously and goes off when a threshold is crossed.
- ⚠️ Interviewer probe: "How do you evaluate this without re-scanning everything?" → it runs as a continuous windowed aggregation in the stream processor, not a repeated query.

> 🎙️ Script: "The entities are simple. The star is the LogEvent — one structured line, carrying a log_id so we can dedupe. Around it there's the Source, which is just metadata about which service and host produced it; the Index or time-bucket, which is how we group events so we can search and age them out efficiently; and the AlertRule, which is a standing condition we evaluate on the live stream. The one that surprises people is the time-bucket — it's not glamorous, but bucketing by time is what makes both retention and search cheap. I'll flesh out the fields in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

Default is REST for control-plane operations and a high-throughput **batch ingest** endpoint (and a gRPC/agent protocol) for the hot path, because per-line HTTP calls would be far too chatty.

**1. Ingest logs — `POST /v1/logs:batch`** (auth: service mTLS or API key)
- WHY POST: it creates new state (appends events). Batched because at 100K/s, one HTTP request per line would melt the gateway — agents buffer ~1–5s and ship batches.
- Key request fields: `events: LogEvent[]` — the batch; `source: string` — service identity (validated against the auth identity, *not* trusted blindly); `log_id: string` per event — for dedupe. The client does NOT send the server receipt time — the server stamps `ingest_ts` so clocks can't be forged.
```
// Request
POST /v1/logs:batch
{ "source": "payments-svc",
  "events": [
    { "log_id": "9f2c-...", "ts": "2026-06-19T14:32:00.123Z", "level": "ERROR",
      "message": "checkout failed: gateway timeout", "trace_id": "abc123", "host": "ip-10-0-1-7" }
  ] }

// Response 202
{ "accepted": 1, "rejected": 0, "ingest_ts": "2026-06-19T14:32:00.480Z" }
```
- Key design decision: returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — we've durably buffered to Kafka but not yet indexed. Acknowledging only after the durable write (not after indexing) is what gives us low producer latency *and* no acknowledged-log loss.

**2. Search logs — `GET /v1/logs/search`** (auth: user JWT)
- WHY GET: it only reads, no state change; cacheable and safe to retry.
- Key query params: `q` — Lucene-style query; `service`, `level` — structured filters; `from`/`to` — time window (drives index pruning); `limit`/`cursor` — pagination. The time window is required — an unbounded search across a year would scan everything.
```
GET /v1/logs/search?q=gateway+timeout&service=payments-svc&level=ERROR&from=2026-06-19T14:15Z&to=2026-06-19T14:32Z&limit=100

// Response 200
{ "hits": 42, "results": [ { "log_id":"9f2c-...", "ts":"...", "message":"checkout failed: gateway timeout", "host":"ip-10-0-1-7" } ], "cursor": "eyJ..." }
```
- Key design decision: the time range prunes to only the relevant time-bucket indexes, so a 15-minute query never touches the 30-day cluster — this is what keeps p99 under 2s.

**3. Create alert rule — `POST /v1/alerts`** (auth: user JWT, role-checked)
- WHY POST: creates a new standing rule.
- Key request fields: `condition` — the windowed expression; `window` — e.g. "1m"; `threshold`; `notify` — channel (PagerDuty/Slack). Server sets `rule_id`, `owner` (from JWT, never client-supplied), and `created_at`.
```
// Request
POST /v1/alerts
{ "name":"payments-error-spike", "condition":"level=ERROR AND service=payments-svc",
  "window":"1m", "threshold":100, "notify":"pagerduty:payments-oncall" }
// Response 201
{ "rule_id":"al_77a2", "owner":"u_555", "status":"ACTIVE", "created_at":"..." }
```
- Key design decision: `owner` is taken from the JWT, never the request — so a user can't create rules attributed to someone else.

> 💬 "Notice ingest returns 202, not 200 — I'm telling the producer 'I've durably got it' the instant it hits the buffer, before it's searchable. That decoupling is the whole reason logging never slows the app down."

> 🎙️ Script: "The API splits cleanly. The hot path is a batched ingest endpoint — agents buffer a second or two of logs and ship them in one call, and I return 202 the moment they're durably buffered, not when they're indexed, so the producer never waits on search. Search is a plain GET with a required time window, because that window is what lets me prune to a handful of time-bucketed indexes instead of scanning everything. And alerts are a small REST resource where the owner comes from the token, not the body, so nobody can spoof identity."

## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

### 6.1 "Ingest logs from many services with minimal latency"

The user is an application that wants to fire a log and move on. The interesting part: accepting at 300K/s without blocking the producer or losing data.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Log agent | A small daemon on each host that tails app logs, buffers a few seconds, and ships batches | App writes to local stdout/file — never blocks on network; agent handles retries | Not direct app→server HTTP (couples app latency to our uptime; a slow ingest stalls prod) | Agent can lose a few seconds of unbuffered logs if the host dies instantly |
| Ingest gateway | A stateless service behind a load balancer that authenticates batches and writes to Kafka | Need a thin, horizontally scalable front door that validates + enqueues fast | Not writing straight to Elasticsearch (indexing is slow, ~10ms/doc; would backpressure producers) | Adds one hop; mitigated by being stateless and trivially scaled |
| Kafka (message buffer) | A durable, partitioned log that holds events in order and replays them to consumers | Absorbs the 3x incident burst and decouples fast ingest from slow indexing; replicated = no loss | Not RabbitMQ (lower sustained throughput, weaker replay); not direct-to-DB (no burst absorption) | Eventual indexing — a log is durable in ~ms but searchable in seconds |

> 🎙️ Narration: "Each host runs a tiny agent that tails the app's log file, so the app just writes locally and never waits on us. The agent batches a second or two and ships to a stateless ingest gateway, which authenticates and writes straight into Kafka — that's a durable, partitioned commit log. I picked Kafka because it's purpose-built to swallow a firehose and replay it, so it absorbs incident bursts and decouples our fast accept from our slower indexing. I rejected writing directly to the search index because indexing is ten-ish milliseconds a document — that would push back on producers and slow prod. The trade-off is a log is durable in milliseconds but searchable in seconds, which is fine."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, partitioned commit log: producers append events, consumers read them in order and can replay. Here it buffers the firehose so nothing is lost when indexing is slow.
> **Partition** — Kafka splits a topic into parallel ordered shards keyed by a field. Here we key by `service` so one service's logs stay ordered and load spreads across brokers.
> **Backpressure** — when a slow downstream forces an upstream to wait. Here Kafka absorbs bursts so producers never feel it.

**Diagram — ingest path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App[App + Log agent]
    App -->|"1: ship batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    subgraph Ingest["Ingest front door"]
        GW -->|"2: auth + validate"| GW2[Validate + key by service]
    end
    GW2 -->|"3: append events"| K[[Kafka topic - partitioned by service]]
    K -.->|"4: ack 202 after durable write"| App
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class App client
    class GW,GW2 svc
    class K queue
```

**Numbered step narration**
1. The agent ships a batch over mTLS. WHY the agent: it decouples the app from our network so logging is fire-and-forget. STATE: events leave the host's buffer. What could go wrong: host dies with unflushed buffer → we accept bounded loss of a few seconds (tunable flush interval).
2. The gateway authenticates and validates the `source` against the auth identity. WHY here: it's the trust boundary — a service can only log as itself. STATE: nothing persisted yet. What could go wrong: bad/oversized batch → reject with 4xx, agent retries valid subset.
3. The gateway appends events to Kafka, partitioned by service. WHY Kafka: durable + replayable + burst-absorbing. STATE: events durably replicated across brokers. What could go wrong: a broker is down → Kafka's replication means another replica serves; producer retries idempotently.
4. Only after the durable write does the gateway return 202. WHY: that's our durability guarantee — we never ack before the data is safe. STATE: producer is done. What could go wrong: ack lost in flight → agent retries, dedupe by `log_id` handles the duplicate.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "The whole trick of ingest is this 202 — I acknowledge the moment it's durable in Kafka, not when it's searchable, so the app never waits on the slow part."

> 🎙️ Script: "Ingest is fire-and-forget by design. An agent on each host batches logs and ships to a stateless gateway that just authenticates and drops events into Kafka, a partitioned durable log. I ack with a 202 the instant Kafka has replicated the write, so producers see millisecond latency and we still never lose an acknowledged log. The one decision that makes this work is decoupling accept from index — Kafka sits in the middle so a slow search cluster can never slow down the apps we're observing."

### 6.2 "Aggregate and durably store logs"

Now the user is the system itself: drain Kafka, and put data where it can be both searched (recently) and archived cheaply (forever-ish).

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indexer (consumer) | A pool of workers that read Kafka and write to the hot search store | Need parallel, replayable consumption that scales with partitions | Not a single consumer (can't keep up at 100K/s) | Must handle duplicates (at-least-once) → dedupe by log_id |
| Elasticsearch (hot store) | A search engine that pre-builds a word→document index, so queries return in ms | Need ranked full-text + structured search over recent data in <2s | Not SQL (LIKE scans every row, no inverted index); not raw object store (no search) | Expensive per GB → only keep 30 days here |
| Object storage (warm/cold) | Cheap, near-infinite durable blob storage (S3-class) for compressed log archives | Need ~200TB/yr at ~1/20th the cost of SSD; 11 nines durability | Not keeping it all in ES (10–50x cost); not deleting (compliance) | Slower to query — must rehydrate or query via an external table engine |

> 🎙️ Narration: "A pool of indexer workers drains Kafka in parallel — one consumer group, partitions spread across workers, so throughput scales with partition count. They write recent logs into Elasticsearch, which is a search engine that pre-builds an inverted index so queries land in milliseconds. But Elasticsearch on SSD is expensive, so I only keep 30 days there. In parallel, a sink writes the raw compressed logs to object storage — S3-class — which is dirt cheap and eleven-nines durable, for the long tail. I rejected keeping everything in Elasticsearch purely on cost: it'd be ten to fifty times more expensive for data almost nobody queries."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Elasticsearch** — a search engine built on an inverted index (a map from each word to the documents containing it). Here it powers sub-second search over the last 30 days.
> **Inverted index** — instead of scanning rows, it stores word→list-of-docs, so "find ERROR in payments" is a lookup, not a scan. Here it's why search is fast.
> **Object storage (S3)** — cheap, virtually unlimited, highly durable blob store. Here it holds the compressed long-term archive at a fraction of SSD cost.
> **Consumer group** — Kafka's way of splitting partitions across a pool of workers so they share the load. Here it lets indexers scale horizontally.

**Diagram — aggregate + store (dual sink)**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka topic]]
    K -->|"1: consume batch"| IDX[Indexer workers]
    subgraph Hot["⚡ Hot path - searchable"]
        IDX -->|"2: dedupe + index"| ES[(Elasticsearch 30d)]
    end
    subgraph Cold["🗄️ Archive path"]
        IDX -->|"3: compress + write"| OBJ[(Object storage 1yr)]
    end
    ES -.->|"4: age out at 30d"| OBJ
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class IDX svc
    class ES,OBJ db
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1)

**Numbered step narration**
1. Indexer workers consume Kafka batches. WHY a consumer group: parallelism scales with partitions, and Kafka tracks offsets so a crashed worker resumes where it left off. STATE: offsets advance only after successful write. Goes wrong: worker crashes mid-batch → it re-reads from last committed offset → duplicates, handled by step 2.
2. Workers dedupe by `log_id` and index into Elasticsearch, into a time-bucketed index. WHY: log_id as document id makes re-indexing idempotent. STATE: event is searchable. Goes wrong: ES rejects (cluster full) → worker pauses, doesn't advance offset, Kafka retains data (no loss).
3. The same workers compress and write raw events to object storage in time-partitioned files. WHY dual-write: hot for search, cold for cheap durability/compliance. STATE: durable archive exists. Goes wrong: object write fails → retry; archive lags but Kafka still holds the data.
4. A retention job drops Elasticsearch indices older than 30 days. WHY time-buckets: dropping a whole index is O(1) vs deleting billions of rows. STATE: hot tier stays bounded; data still in object storage.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "I fan out from Kafka into two sinks — a fast expensive one for 30 days of search and a cheap durable one for the year — so I never pay search prices for archive data."

> 🎙️ Script: "Once logs are in Kafka, a pool of indexer workers drains them in parallel. Each event goes two places: into Elasticsearch for fast search, but only for 30 days, and into object storage compressed for the long-term archive. Elasticsearch gives me sub-second search via its inverted index; object storage gives me near-infinite cheap durability. I age indices out by simply dropping the whole time-bucket index, which is instant. The key decision is the dual sink — separating 'searchable' from 'durable' is what keeps both the latency and the cost in check."

### 6.3 "Search and filter logs"

The on-call engineer queries during an incident. Interesting part: sub-2s over billions of docs by never touching more index than the time window requires.

↳ builds on §6.2 — the only new component is a **query/search service** in front of Elasticsearch; no new datastore.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Query service | A stateless API that parses the query, prunes to relevant indices, fans out to ES shards, merges results | Centralizes auth, query limits, and index pruning; protects ES from bad queries | Not querying ES directly from clients (no auth, no guardrails, easy to DoS) | One extra hop (~ms) |

> 🎙️ Narration: "Search goes through a stateless query service that does three jobs: it enforces auth and per-tenant query limits, it prunes the time range down to just the time-bucket indices that overlap the window, and it fans the query across Elasticsearch shards and merges the top hits. I don't let clients hit Elasticsearch directly because a single unbounded query could take the cluster down. The trade-off is a millisecond hop, which is nothing next to the safety it buys."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Shard** — a horizontal slice of an Elasticsearch index living on one node; queries fan out across shards in parallel. Here sharding is how a single query stays fast over billions of docs.
> **Index pruning** — using the time window to skip indices that can't contain matches. Here it turns a year-scale problem into a 15-minute one.

**Diagram — search path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[Engineer / dashboard]
    U -->|"1: GET search + time window"| QS[Query service]
    QS -->|"2: prune to time buckets"| QS2[Pick matching indices]
    QS2 -->|"3: fan out query"| ES[(Elasticsearch shards)]
    ES -->|"4: merge top hits"| QS
    QS -->|"5: return hits"| U
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class U client
    class QS,QS2 svc
    class ES db
```
↳ reuses existing: Elasticsearch (from §6.2)

**Numbered step narration**
1. Engineer submits a query with a required time window. WHY required: an unbounded query would scan all 30 days. STATE: none. Goes wrong: missing window → reject with a default-to-15-min hint.
2. Query service prunes to the overlapping time-bucket indices. WHY: skips 99% of data. STATE: candidate index set chosen. Goes wrong: very wide window → enforce a max and paginate.
3. It fans the query across the shards of those indices in parallel. WHY shards: parallelism keeps latency flat as data grows. STATE: partial results gathering. Goes wrong: one slow shard → use a per-shard timeout and return partial + a "results may be incomplete" flag.
4–5. Results are merged, ranked, paginated, and returned. Goes wrong: huge result set → cursor-based pagination, never load it all.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "The time window isn't a filter, it's the performance trick — it prunes the search down to a couple of indices before Elasticsearch ever runs."

> 🎙️ Script: "Search is a stateless query service in front of Elasticsearch. The engineer always supplies a time window, and I use it to prune to just the time-bucket indices that overlap — so a 15-minute query during an outage never touches the full 30 days. Then I fan out across shards in parallel and merge the top hits with per-shard timeouts so one slow shard can't hang the whole query. The single idea that makes this fast is time-bucketing plus pruning: I turn 'search a year' into 'search two small indices.'"

### 6.4 "Analyze and alert on logs"

The system continuously watches the stream and pages a human on a spike — without re-querying the index.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stream processor (Flink) | An engine that runs continuous windowed aggregations over the live event stream | Alerts need second-level latency on a moving window — exactly stream processing | Not periodic ES queries (minutes of lag, hammers the cluster); not a cron (misses bursts between runs) | Operational complexity of running Flink; another consumer of Kafka |
| Notifier | A service that dedupes firing alerts and routes to PagerDuty/Slack | Need rate-limited, deduped paging so one spike doesn't send 1000 pages | Not paging straight from Flink (no dedupe/routing logic) | Slight added latency for dedupe window |

> 🎙️ Narration: "Alerting runs on a stream processor — Flink — which consumes the same Kafka stream and maintains continuous windowed counts, like 'errors per minute for payments.' When a window crosses a threshold, it emits an alert event to a notifier, which dedupes (so a sustained spike pages you once, not a thousand times) and routes to PagerDuty or Slack. I rejected periodically querying Elasticsearch because that's minutes of lag and it hammers the search cluster exactly when it's busiest during an incident."

**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Flink** — a stream processor that runs standing computations over an unbounded event stream, keeping state in windows. Here it computes live error-rate aggregations for alerting.
> **Windowed aggregation** — counting/aggregating events over a rolling time window (e.g. last 1 min). Here it's how we detect spikes without re-scanning history.

**Diagram — alerting path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[[Kafka topic]]
    K -->|"1: consume stream"| FL[Flink - windowed counts]
    FL -->|"2: threshold crossed"| NT[Notifier - dedupe + route]
    NT -->|"3: page on-call"| PD[PagerDuty / Slack]
    RULES[(AlertRule store)] -.->|"0: load rules"| FL
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2350,stroke:#6B46C1,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class K queue
    class FL,NT svc
    class RULES db
    class PD ext
```
↳ reuses existing: Kafka (from §6.1)

**Numbered step narration**
1. Flink consumes the live stream (its own consumer group, independent of indexers). WHY separate group: alerting must not compete with or wait on indexing. STATE: in-memory window counters update. Goes wrong: Flink crashes → it restores from a checkpoint and replays from the last offset, so no missed window.
2. When a window crosses a rule threshold, Flink emits an alert event. WHY in-stream: second-level latency, no index round trip. STATE: alert candidate produced. Goes wrong: clock skew on event time → use event-time windows with a bounded watermark for late data.
3. The notifier dedupes within a cooldown and routes to the channel. WHY: prevents alert storms. STATE: a page is sent + recorded. Goes wrong: PagerDuty down → fall back to a secondary channel and retry with backoff.

**💬 Say-while-drawing:** "Alerts ride the same Kafka stream as a separate consumer, so alerting is instant and never fights with indexing or search."

> 🎙️ Script: "Alerting is a stream-processing job on Flink reading the same Kafka topic as its own consumer group. It keeps rolling per-rule counts in memory — error rate per minute per service — and the moment a window crosses a threshold it emits an alert. A notifier dedupes so a sustained incident pages you once, then routes to PagerDuty. The decision here is doing this on the stream rather than by querying the index: it's seconds instead of minutes, and it doesn't pile load onto the search cluster during the exact incident you're alerting about."

### Final (high-level)

The reconciled union of all four slices — every component appears with its original name and color.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App[App + Log agent]
    App -->|"1: ship batch"| GW[Ingest gateway]
    GW -->|"2: append"| K[[Kafka - partitioned by service]]

    subgraph WritePath["🔄 Index + Archive path"]
        K -->|"3: consume"| IDX[Indexer workers]
        IDX -->|"4: dedupe + index"| ES[(Elasticsearch 30d)]
        IDX -->|"5: compress + write"| OBJ[(Object storage 1yr)]
    end

    subgraph AlertPath["🚨 Alerting path"]
        K -->|"6: consume stream"| FL[Flink windowed counts]
        FL -->|"7: threshold"| NT[Notifier]
        NT -->|"8: page"| PD[PagerDuty / Slack]
    end

    subgraph ReadPath["⚡ Search path"]
        U[Engineer] -->|"9: search + window"| QS[Query service]
        QS -->|"10: prune + fan out"| ES
    end

    ES -.->|"11: age out 30d"| OBJ
    RULES[(AlertRule store)] -.->|"load"| FL
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    classDef ext fill:#3a2350,stroke:#6B46C1,stroke-width:2px,color:#e6edf3
    class App,U client
    class GW,IDX,FL,NT,QS svc
    class K queue
    class ES,OBJ,RULES db
    class PD ext
```

Load-bearing schema: `Elasticsearch` indices are named `logs-{service}-{yyyymmddhh}` (the time bucket = retention + pruning key); `Kafka` is partitioned by `service` (ordering + load spread); `Object storage` paths are `s3://logs/{service}/{date}/{hour}.parquet.gz`.

> 🎙️ Script: "Read top to bottom: apps hand logs to an agent, which batches to a stateless gateway, which durably buffers them in Kafka — that's the firehose-absorbing spine. From Kafka, three independent consumers fan out: indexer workers that write 30 days into Elasticsearch and the full archive into object storage; a Flink job that watches the stream for alert conditions and pages on-call; and on the read side a query service that prunes by time window and fans queries across Elasticsearch. The one principle holding it all together is decoupling via Kafka — accept fast and durably once, then let search, archive, and alerting each consume at their own pace."

> 🤝 Checkpoint: "That's the skeleton end to end. The three hardest parts are the ingest firehose under burst, the storage tiering economics, and search at scale. Want me to go deepest on the write/ingest path, or on search?"

## 7. Data Model & Storage

**PART A — Entity table**

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key noted) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LogEvent | `log_id` (PK/doc id), `ts`, `service`, `level`, `message` (full-text), `trace_id`, `host` | Elasticsearch (hot) + Object storage (archive) | `service` + time bucket | Eventual (searchable in ~seconds) |
| Source | `service` (PK), `env`, `region`, `first_seen`, `flags` | Postgres (control plane) | `service` | Strong |
| Index/TimeBucket | `index_name` (PK), `service`, `window_start`, `doc_count`, `state` | Elasticsearch metadata + Postgres | `index_name` | Strong (metadata) |
| AlertRule | `rule_id` (PK), `owner`, `condition`, `window`, `threshold`, `notify`, `status` | Postgres | `rule_id` | Strong |

**PART B — Storage decision cards**

**🗄️ Kafka — used for: the durable ingest buffer (the LogEvent firehose)**
1. What it is: a durable, partitioned, append-only commit log — producers append events and consumers read them in order and can replay from any point.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: we need to accept 300K writes/s during bursts and decouple a fast durable accept from slow downstream indexing; Kafka's partitioned replicated log is purpose-built for exactly this and gives replay so a crashed consumer loses nothing.
3. What we considered instead: **RabbitMQ** — great for task queues but lower sustained throughput and weaker long-retention replay; **Kinesis** — viable on AWS but per-shard limits and cost get awkward at this rate and it's vendor-locked; **direct-to-DB** — no burst absorption, the DB becomes the bottleneck on the worst day.
4. Trade-off we accept: a log is durable in milliseconds but only searchable in seconds (indexing lag).
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I put Kafka in the middle because logging is a firehose and I need to accept it fast and durably, then let indexing catch up. Rabbit can't sustain the throughput and a direct DB write would backpressure prod. The cost is a few seconds before a log is searchable, which is totally fine for logs."

**🗄️ Elasticsearch — used for: the hot, searchable LogEvents (last 30 days)**
1. What it is: a search engine built on an inverted index — it pre-maps every word to the documents containing it, so text + structured queries return in milliseconds.
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: the core read is "full-text + filtered search over recent logs in under 2 seconds," and only an inverted index can do that; a row scan over billions of logs is hopeless.
3. What we considered instead: **SQL with LIKE** — scans every row, no ranking, dies at this scale; **ClickHouse** — fantastic for analytical aggregations but weaker at ad-hoc full-text search and tailing; **a hand-rolled index** — months of work, no relevance ranking.
4. Trade-off we accept: it's expensive per GB on SSD, so we can only afford ~30 days hot.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Search is the whole point on the read side, so I use Elasticsearch — it's an inverted index, which means querying is a lookup not a scan. SQL LIKE would scan billions of rows. The catch is it's pricey, so I only keep 30 days there and tier the rest to object storage."

**🗄️ Object storage (S3-class) — used for: the long-term compressed archive of all LogEvents (1 year)**
1. What it is: cheap, virtually unlimited, extremely durable blob storage that stores files (here, compressed time-partitioned log batches).
2. Why we picked it — the access pattern: we must retain ~200TB/yr durably and cheaply for compliance and occasional deep queries, where instant search isn't needed; object storage is ~1/20th the cost of SSD and offers eleven-nines durability.
3. What we considered instead: **keep everything in Elasticsearch** — 10–50x the cost for data almost no one queries; **a data warehouse** — overkill and pricier for raw archive; **deleting old logs** — violates retention/compliance.
4. Trade-off we accept: archived data isn't instantly searchable — we query it via an external table engine (Athena/Presto over Parquet) or rehydrate, which is slower.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Everything older than 30 days goes to object storage as compressed Parquet — it's dirt cheap and incredibly durable. I considered keeping it all in Elasticsearch but that's ten-plus times the cost for data nobody touches. If someone needs old data, we query it with Athena or rehydrate it — slower, but rare."

**🗄️ Postgres — used for: control-plane metadata (Sources, AlertRules, index registry)**
1. What it is: a relational database with strict schemas and transactions.
2. Why we picked it: this is small, low-volume, relational config data that needs strong consistency (an alert rule must be exactly what the user set); it's a classic Postgres job.
3. What we considered instead: **storing config in Elasticsearch** — wrong tool, no transactional guarantees; **a KV store** — fine but we want relational queries (rules by owner, sources by region).
4. Trade-off we accept: not horizontally huge — but config volume is tiny, so it never needs to be.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The metadata — who's logging, what the alert rules are — lives in Postgres because it's small, relational, and needs to be exactly right. The logs themselves never touch it."

**PART C — Per-operation consistency summary**

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accept a log (durable write) | Kafka | Strong (replicated ack) | We must never lose an acknowledged log |
| Make a log searchable | Elasticsearch | Eventual (~seconds) | A few seconds of index lag is fine for logs |
| Read-your-own-write (just-logged → searchable) | ES | Eventual, bounded ~30s | We tell users logs are searchable within ~30s; for "did it land?" they can check ingest ack, not search |
| Create/update an AlertRule | Postgres | Strong | A rule must take effect exactly as set, immediately |
| Query the archive | Object storage | Eventual / read-after-close | Archive files are immutable once written |

> 🎙️ Script: "Storage is four stores, each doing one job. Kafka is the durable buffer that guarantees no acknowledged log is lost. Elasticsearch holds 30 days hot for fast search via its inverted index. Object storage holds a year of compressed archive at a fraction of the cost. And Postgres holds the small control-plane metadata — sources and alert rules — where I need strong consistency. The one thing I'll flag: I considered keeping everything in Elasticsearch for simplicity, but I rejected it on cost — it'd be more than ten times pricier for archive data nobody queries, so tiering to object storage is the senior call here."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

🆘 If you get stuck: go back to the firehose. "The binding constraint is 300K writes/s that must be durable and eventually searchable — let me reason forward from that bottleneck."

### How do we ingest 300K logs/s without losing data or blocking producers?

**#### Bad: Synchronous write straight to the search index**
🗣️ What this is in plain words: every app sends each log directly to Elasticsearch and waits for "done."
Approach: app → HTTP → Elasticsearch index call, blocking until indexed.
Why people try this: it's the simplest mental model — "store the log."
⚠️ What breaks: ES indexing is ~5–10ms/doc and chokes under burst; at 300K/s the index can't keep up, so producer threads block, the app's request latency balloons, and logging takes down prod. Connection pools exhaust in seconds.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: any real burst — the app must never wait on indexing.

**#### Good: Buffer in Kafka, index asynchronously**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: it coupled producer latency to indexing speed; we break that by inserting a durable buffer so accept and index run at different speeds.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: apps drop logs into a fast durable mailbox (Kafka); separate workers pick them up and index at their own pace.
Approach: gateway writes to Kafka (replicated, acked in ms), returns 202; an indexer consumer group drains Kafka into ES. Kafka partitioned by `service`.
Why this seemed reasonable: it's the canonical decoupling pattern.
⚠️ What breaks: a single hot service (a service in a crash loop logging millions of errors) all maps to one partition → that partition and its consumer lag while others idle — a hot-partition problem.
🔁 What forces the upgrade: incident log storms concentrated on one service.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GW[Ingest gateway] -->|"write"| K[[Kafka by service]]
    K -->|"consume"| IDX[Indexer group]
    IDX -->|"index"| ES[(Elasticsearch)]
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef queue fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef db fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class GW,IDX svc
    class K queue
    class ES db
```

**#### Great: Composite partition key + bulk indexing + backpressure**
↩️ What the previous tier got wrong: keying purely by service created hot partitions; we fix it by spreading a hot service across sub-partitions and adding bulk + backpressure.
🗣️ What this is in plain words: we split a noisy service's logs across several lanes instead of one, batch writes for efficiency, and let Kafka hold the overflow when indexing falls behind.
Approach: partition key = `service + hash(host) % N` so one service spreads across N partitions; indexers use ES **bulk API** (hundreds of docs per request, ~10x throughput); when ES is saturated, indexers stop advancing offsets and Kafka retains data (backpressure with zero loss). Dedupe by `log_id` as the ES document id makes retries idempotent.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: one ES node ingests ~10K docs/s single-doc but ~50K+/s via bulk; at 300K/s peak that's ~6 bulk-indexing nodes — versus ~30 without bulk. Kafka at 100MB/s/broker covers 150MB/s peak with 3+ brokers for HA.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Indexer crashes mid-batch | Re-reads from last committed offset | Idempotent index by `log_id` → no dup |
| ES cluster saturated | Indexers stop advancing offsets | Kafka retains (hours of buffer); backpressure, no loss |
| Duplicate from at-least-once | Same `log_id` re-indexed | Overwrites same doc, not a new one |
| Broker dies | Replica takes over | Replication factor 3; producer retries |
| Hot service log storm | One service floods | Composite key spreads across N partitions |

> 🎙️ Script: "The ingest deep dive is all about decoupling and not creating a new bottleneck. Bad is writing straight to the index — it blocks producers and dies under burst. Good puts Kafka in the middle so accept and index run independently. But keying Kafka purely by service creates a hot partition when one service melts down and floods logs, so Great spreads each service across several partitions with a composite key, uses bulk indexing for about a 5x throughput win, and relies on Kafka backpressure so if Elasticsearch saturates we just buffer instead of dropping. Dedupe is by log_id as the document id, so retries are idempotent."
> 🧠 If they ask "what if Kafka itself fills up?": "Kafka is sized for hours of buffer at peak, and we alarm on consumer lag. If it's truly sustained, we scale indexers or shed the lowest-priority log levels (drop DEBUG first), never the durable ERROR logs."

### How do we keep storage cheap while staying searchable? (tiering)

**#### Bad: Everything in Elasticsearch forever**
🗣️ Plain words: keep all logs in the fast search cluster permanently.
Approach: never delete from ES.
Why people try this: everything's searchable, one system.
⚠️ What breaks: ~200TB/yr on SSD-backed ES nodes is 10–50x the cost of object storage; the cluster grows unboundedly, shards multiply, and query + recovery times degrade.
🔁 Forces upgrade: the bill and the operational weight.

**#### Good: Hot/warm/cold tiers within Elasticsearch**
↩️ Previous tier wrong: one tier paid SSD prices for cold data; we fix it by moving aging data to cheaper nodes.
🗣️ Plain words: recent logs on fast machines, older logs on cheaper slower machines, all still in ES.
Approach: ES ILM (Index Lifecycle Management) moves indices hot→warm→cold across node tiers by age.
⚠️ What breaks: even "cold" ES nodes cost far more than object storage, and you still run the cluster for year-old data nobody queries.
🔁 Forces upgrade: cost of a year of cold ES nodes.

**#### Great: 30-day ES + object-storage archive queried on demand**
↩️ Previous tier wrong: kept cold data in the (expensive) cluster; we fix it by evicting it to object storage entirely.
🗣️ Plain words: keep only 30 days in the search cluster; everything older lives as cheap compressed files we can still query when we rarely need to.
Approach: dual-write at index time (ES + Parquet on S3); ES indices dropped at 30 days (O(1) per time-bucket); archive queried via Athena/Presto over Parquet, or rehydrated into a temporary ES index for a deep investigation.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: 30 days hot ×3 replicas ≈ 40TB on SSD; the other ~165TB/yr on object storage at ~1/20th $/GB → roughly an order-of-magnitude total cost cut vs all-hot.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Need a 6-month-old log | Not in ES | Query Parquet via Athena, or rehydrate that day's file |
| Archive write fails | Object lags | Retry; Kafka still holds source data |
| Accidental index drop | Hot data gone | Snapshots to object storage; restore |
| Compliance hold | Must keep > 1yr | Lifecycle policy extends archive retention cheaply |

> 🎙️ Script: "Tiering is the cost story. Bad keeps everything in Elasticsearch — ten to fifty times too expensive. Good moves aging data to cheaper ES nodes but you're still paying cluster prices for stale data. Great keeps just 30 days hot and writes everything to compressed Parquet on object storage, dropping whole time-bucket indices instantly at retention. If someone needs old data — rarely — we query the Parquet with Athena or rehydrate one day's file. That single move cuts total storage cost by roughly an order of magnitude."
> 🧠 If they ask "isn't querying the archive painful?": "Yes, it's seconds-to-minutes, not sub-second — but 90% of queries hit the last 24 hours, so we optimize for the common case and accept slow-but-cheap for the rare deep dig."

### How do we make search fast and not let one bad query take the cluster down?

**#### Good: Time-bucketed indices + index pruning**
🗣️ Plain words: store logs in per-hour/per-service buckets so a query only opens the buckets in its time range.
Approach: index naming `logs-{service}-{yyyymmddhh}`; query service prunes to overlapping buckets before hitting ES.
⚠️ What breaks: a user runs an unbounded or 30-day wildcard query → fans across every shard, saturates the cluster, slows everyone.

**#### Great: Pruning + per-shard timeouts + query guardrails**
↩️ Previous tier wrong: no protection against a pathological query; we add limits and timeouts.
🗣️ Plain words: cap how much a single query can scan, time out slow shards, and return partial results rather than hanging.
Approach: enforce a max time window + result limit; per-shard timeout returns partial hits flagged "incomplete"; expensive aggregations routed to a separate node pool so ad-hoc analytics can't starve incident search; cursor pagination for large result sets.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: a 15-min window prunes to ~1–2 hourly indices out of 720 (30d×24h) → ~0.2% of the data scanned, keeping p99 well under 2s.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Unbounded query | Would scan all | Reject / clamp to max window |
| One slow shard | Could hang query | Per-shard timeout → partial + flag |
| Heavy aggregation | Starves search | Separate node pool for analytics |
| Huge result set | OOM risk | Cursor pagination |

> 🎙️ Script: "Search speed comes from time-bucketing: I name indices by service and hour, so a 15-minute query prunes to one or two indices out of 720 — a fraction of a percent of the data. To stop a bad query from taking the cluster down, I clamp the max window and result size, time out slow shards and return partial results, and route heavy aggregations to a separate node pool so ad-hoc analytics never starve incident search."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not let users query freely?": "Because one unbounded query during an incident could saturate the cluster for everyone — guardrails protect the shared resource when it matters most."

### How do we alert in seconds without hammering the search cluster?

**#### Good: Periodic queries against Elasticsearch**
🗣️ Plain words: a cron runs the alert query every minute.
⚠️ What breaks: minutes of latency, and it loads the search cluster during incidents — exactly the wrong time; misses sub-minute bursts.

**#### Great: Stream processing on Kafka (Flink) with checkpointing**
↩️ Previous tier wrong: polling the index is slow and adds load; we move evaluation onto the live stream.
🗣️ Plain words: a job continuously watches the log stream and keeps running counts, firing instantly when a threshold trips.
Approach: Flink consumes Kafka as its own group, maintains event-time windowed aggregates with watermarks (to handle late/out-of-order logs), checkpoints state to object storage for crash recovery, emits alerts to a notifier that dedupes within a cooldown.
🔢 Decision-forcing math: stream eval is ~1–10s end-to-end vs ~60s+ for a 1-min poll cycle — 6x+ faster detection, zero added ES load.
✅ Failure matrix:

| Scenario | What happens | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Flink crashes | State lost? | Restore from checkpoint, replay from offset |
| Late/out-of-order logs | Window miscount | Event-time windows + bounded watermark |
| Alert storm | 1000 pages | Notifier dedupe + cooldown |
| Notifier channel down | Page lost | Secondary channel + retry/backoff |

> 🎙️ Script: "Alerting runs on Flink over the Kafka stream as a separate consumer, keeping windowed counts in memory and firing the instant a threshold trips — seconds, not minutes, and zero extra load on the search cluster. I use event-time windows with watermarks so out-of-order logs still count correctly, and Flink checkpoints its state so a crash just replays from the last offset. A notifier dedupes so a sustained spike pages you once."
> 🧠 If they ask "why not just query ES on a timer?": "Polling is minute-scale and piles load on the search cluster during the exact incident you're alerting about — streaming is faster and isolates the load."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

**9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean)**
- Ingest path: target **99.99% (four nines)** = under ~52 minutes downtime/year. Achieved via a stateless gateway behind a load balancer across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in one region, so if one building loses power another takes over automatically) and Kafka with replication factor 3 across AZs.
- Search path: **99.9% (three nines)** = ~8.7 hours/year — search being briefly degraded is painful but not data-losing; ES replicas across AZs.
- Durability of acknowledged logs: **~99.999999% (eight nines)** via Kafka 3x replication + object storage 11-nines archive.

**9B — Per-dependency failure table**

| Component | What breaks if it's down | Graceful degradation (what the user sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingest gateway | Can't accept new logs | Agents buffer locally and retry; brief gap, no permanent loss | Auto-scale / restart behind LB |
| Kafka | No durable accept | Gateway returns 503; agents buffer on host | Failover to replicas; restore brokers |
| Indexer workers | New logs not searchable | Logs still durable in Kafka; search shows up to ~minutes stale | Scale consumers; they replay from offset |
| Elasticsearch | Search unavailable | "Recent search degraded"; ingest + archive unaffected | Failover replicas; rehydrate if needed |
| Object storage | Archive writes fail | No user-facing impact short term; Kafka holds source | Retry; backfill from Kafka |
| Flink (alerting) | Alerts stop firing | Dashboards still work; on-call loses auto-pages | Restore from checkpoint, replay |
| Postgres (metadata) | Can't edit rules/sources | Existing rules keep running (cached in Flink) | Failover to replica |

**9C — RPO / RTO per data class**
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if it all crashes right now. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long until service is working again.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acknowledged logs (in Kafka) | ≈0 | minutes | 3x sync replication; consumers replay from offset |
| Hot search index (ES) | minutes (rebuildable) | minutes–hours | Snapshots to object storage; or re-index from Kafka/archive |
| Archive (object storage) | ≈0 | seconds | 11-nines durable, immutable files |
| Metadata (Postgres) | seconds | minutes | Synchronous replica + PITR backups |

The search index is deliberately treated as **rebuildable** — if ES is lost, we re-index from Kafka (recent) and the Parquet archive (older), so we don't need expensive zero-RPO replication on the search tier.

**9D — Cost breakdown**
Top 3 drivers: (1) **Elasticsearch hot nodes (SSD + RAM)** — the single most expensive line; ~40TB hot ×3 replicas on memory-heavy nodes. (2) **Object storage + egress** — ~200TB/yr is cheap to store but archive queries (Athena scans) and any cross-region transfer add up. (3) **Kafka brokers** — replicated SSD storage for the buffer. Rough order of magnitude: tens of thousands of dollars/month, dominated by the ES tier. The single biggest lever: **shorten hot retention** (30→14 days roughly halves the ES cost) and **drop DEBUG logs at ingest** (often 50%+ of volume).

> 🎙️ Script: "When things fail, the user experience degrades gracefully because Kafka is the durable spine. If the search cluster falls over, ingest and archiving keep going and we just rebuild the index from Kafka and the archive — that's why I treat the index as rebuildable rather than paying for zero-RPO there. If indexers lag, logs are still safe and just show up a bit late. The only thing a user truly can't get back is a few seconds of unbuffered logs on a host that dies instantly. Cost-wise, the Elasticsearch hot tier dominates, so the biggest knobs are hot-retention length and dropping low-value DEBUG logs at the door."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once delivery + dedupe vs exactly-once**
1. What we chose and why: at-least-once with `log_id` dedupe — far simpler and faster at 300K/s.
2. What we gave up: occasional duplicate logs that we must dedupe on index/read.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: like mailing two copies of a letter to be sure one arrives, then ignoring the duplicate — simpler than a guaranteed single delivery.
4. When this reverses: if logs drove billing or exactly-once audit math, we'd pay for transactional exactly-once with idempotent sinks.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I went at-least-once because exactly-once across this pipeline kills throughput; logs tolerate dedupe by id, so it's the right call unless logs become financial records."

**🔀 Decision: 30-day hot ES + object-storage archive vs all-in-Elasticsearch**
1. What we chose and why: tiered storage — an order-of-magnitude cheaper.
2. What we gave up: old data isn't instantly searchable (slower Athena/rehydrate path).
3. 🗣️ Plain words: keep this month's files on your desk, last year's in the basement — cheap, but the basement takes longer.
4. When this reverses: if a regulation required *all* logs instantly searchable for a year, we'd grow the cold ES tier and eat the cost.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "I keep 30 days hot and archive the rest cheaply, because 90% of queries hit the last day. If everything had to be instantly searchable for a year, I'd reverse it and pay for it."

**🔀 Decision: Kafka buffer (async indexing) vs synchronous write to the index**
1. What we chose and why: async via Kafka — protects producers and absorbs bursts.
2. What we gave up: a few seconds of indexing lag (not instantly searchable).
3. 🗣️ Plain words: a mailbox between sender and sorter, so the sender never waits on the sorter.
4. When this reverses: if the use case demanded truly instant searchability of every log (sub-second), we'd need a much pricier always-keeping-up index and still probably keep the buffer.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "The Kafka buffer means apps never wait on indexing and bursts can't drop logs; the only cost is a few seconds of search lag, which is invisible for logging."

**🔀 Decision: Stream processing (Flink) for alerts vs periodic index queries**
1. What we chose and why: Flink — second-level alerts, no load on the search cluster.
2. What we gave up: operational complexity of running a stateful stream processor.
3. 🗣️ Plain words: a smoke detector that's always watching vs walking through the house every minute to check.
4. When this reverses: if alerts could tolerate minutes of latency and volume were low, a simple scheduled query would be cheaper to operate.
5. 🗣️ How to say it: "Flink gives me seconds-level alerting without hammering Elasticsearch; it's more to operate, but for a logging system alerting is core, so it's worth it."

> 🎙️ Script: "My least-certain calls: at-least-once with dedupe instead of exactly-once — right until logs become billing records; tiered storage instead of all-hot — right until everything must be instantly searchable for a year; the Kafka buffer — costs a few seconds of search lag for huge resilience; and Flink for alerting — more ops but core to the product. Every one of these traces back to the same principle: optimize the write firehose and storage cost, because reads are cheap and a few seconds of lag is acceptable for logs."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two indexer workers process the same log after a crash — do we get duplicates?"**
The mechanism: at-least-once means a crashed worker replays from its last committed Kafka offset, so some events re-process. We use `log_id` as the Elasticsearch document id, so re-indexing the same event overwrites the same document rather than creating a new one.
🗣️ In plain words: if the same log shows up twice, it just lands in the same slot and overwrites itself — no duplicate.
💬 One-liner: "Dedupe is free because log_id is the document id — a replay overwrites, it doesn't duplicate."

**❓ "What happens when Elasticsearch goes down during an incident?"**
The mechanism: ingest and archiving are independent of ES — logs keep flowing into Kafka and object storage. Search degrades (we surface "recent search unavailable"), but no data is lost; we fail over to ES replicas across AZs, and worst case re-index from Kafka and the Parquet archive since the index is rebuildable.
🗣️ In plain words: search might be down for a bit, but no logs are lost and we can rebuild the search index from the buffer and archive.
💬 One-liner: "ES is rebuildable — losing it costs search, never data."

**❓ "How do you handle a log storm — one service crash-looping and flooding logs?"**
The mechanism: pure service-keyed partitioning would hot-spot one Kafka partition, so we key by `service + hash(host) % N` to spread a noisy service across N partitions and consumers. Kafka absorbs the burst (hours of buffer), and if indexing still can't keep up we shed the lowest log levels (DEBUG) first, never ERROR.
🗣️ In plain words: we split a noisy service across several lanes so it can't clog one, and the buffer soaks up the spike.
💬 One-liner: "Composite partition key spreads the storm; Kafka buffers it; we drop DEBUG before we ever drop errors."

**❓ "Can you lose an acknowledged log? What's your durability guarantee?"**
The mechanism: we return 202 only after Kafka has replicated the write across 3 brokers in different AZs, so an acknowledged log survives a broker (or AZ) failure. The only loss window is a host dying with unflushed agent buffer — bounded by the flush interval (~seconds), and tunable to near-zero with synchronous flushing at a latency cost.
🗣️ In plain words: once we say "got it," it's on three machines in different buildings — it's not going away.
💬 One-liner: "Ack means replicated 3x across AZs — eight-nines durable."

**❓ "How do you make sure a search returns in under 2 seconds over billions of logs?"**
The mechanism: indices are time-bucketed by hour and service, so a 15-minute query prunes to ~1–2 indices out of 720 — well under 1% of the data. We fan out across shards in parallel and merge top hits, with per-shard timeouts so one slow shard returns partial rather than hanging.
🗣️ In plain words: we file logs by hour, so a recent query only opens a couple of folders instead of the whole cabinet.
💬 One-liner: "Time-bucketing turns 'search a year' into 'search two indices.'"

**❓ "How do you stop a service from logging as another service, or a user forging an alert owner?"**
The mechanism: ingest is authenticated via mTLS/API key and the claimed `source` is validated against the authenticated identity, so a service can only log as itself. On the control plane, an AlertRule's `owner` is taken from the JWT, never the request body, so identity can't be spoofed.
🗣️ In plain words: you can only log as who you actually are, and you can't create rules pretending to be someone else.
💬 One-liner: "Identity comes from the credential, never the payload."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut cost?"**
The mechanism: the Elasticsearch hot tier (SSD + RAM, ~40TB ×3 replicas) dominates the bill, not the 200TB archive (object storage is ~1/20th the price). Biggest levers: shorten hot retention (30→14 days ≈ halves ES cost) and drop DEBUG logs at ingest (often 50%+ of volume).
🗣️ In plain words: the fast search machines cost the most; keeping less data hot and throwing away noisy debug logs saves the most.
💬 One-liner: "Hot retention length is the cost dial — shorten it, and drop DEBUG at the door."

**❓ "How would you add distributed tracing on top of this later?"**
The mechanism: logs already carry a `trace_id`; we'd add a spans index keyed by `trace_id` and a service to assemble spans into a trace timeline, reusing the same ingest/Kafka pipeline. No core change — it's a new consumer and a new index.
🗣️ In plain words: the hooks are already there — we'd just add a new reader that stitches together everything sharing a trace id.
💬 One-liner: "trace_id is already in every log — tracing is a new consumer, not a rebuild."

**❓ "Can the alerting miss a spike if logs arrive out of order?"**
The mechanism: Flink uses event-time windows with a bounded watermark, so it waits a short grace period for late/out-of-order logs before closing a window. Logs later than the watermark are counted in a late-data side output rather than silently dropped.
🗣️ In plain words: we wait a few seconds for stragglers before deciding a minute's count, so out-of-order logs still count.
💬 One-liner: "Event-time windows with watermarks make ordering not matter for alerts."

**❓ "Why Kafka and not just a bigger database?"**
The mechanism: a database can't absorb a 300K/s replicated write burst and replay it to multiple independent consumers (indexer, archiver, alerting) — Kafka is a partitioned, replayable log built exactly for fan-out + burst absorption. The DB would be the single bottleneck on the worst day.
🗣️ In plain words: Kafka is a shared firehose-buffer that many readers can drink from at their own speed; a database can't do that.
💬 One-liner: "Kafka decouples one durable write into three independent consumers — a DB can't."

> 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary: "A logging system is a write firehose that has to double as a search engine. The design splits into two halves. The read half is Elasticsearch holding 30 days of logs in a time-bucketed inverted index, fronted by a query service that prunes by time window so a 15-minute query touches almost no data and comes back in under two seconds. The write half is the hard part: thousands of services fire logs through a stateless gateway into Kafka, a durable partitioned buffer that absorbs 300K-a-second bursts and acks in milliseconds, and the hardest problem there — never losing a log while never blocking a producer — is solved by acknowledging only after a 3x replicated durable write and indexing asynchronously. The two halves connect through Kafka: indexer workers, an archiver to cheap object storage, and a Flink alerting job all consume the same stream independently. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: accept fast and durably once, then let search, archive, and alerting each catch up at their own pace."
